symptom: 用户收到邮件出现数小时延迟，或邮件从收件箱消失后数小时重新出现；Received 头中含 'MailboxResubmission by AntispamTT'；EMT 中出现 STOREDRIVER DELIVER 或 STOREDRIVER DUPLICATEDELIVER 事件（custom_data 含 AntispamTT）
rootCause: ZAP（Zero-hour auto purge）/ Reverse ZAP 触发：EOP 最初将邮件判定为 phish/spam 并移至隔离，随后检测到该判定为误报（false positive），Reverse ZAP 通过 SAFETYNET RESUBMIT 将邮件重新投递回收件箱，导致用户感知到数小时延迟或邮件重复出现
solution: 1. 在 EMT 中查找 SAFETYNET RESUBMIT 事件确认 ZAP 触发时间线；2. 检查 AntispamTT 相关的 STOREDRIVER DELIVER/DUPLICATEDELIVER 事件及 custom_data；3. 在隔离区（Quarantine）查找原始 ZAP 事件（redelivery 后记录仍保留，可供管理员查询）；4. 告知用户这是正常的 Reverse ZAP 行为（误报修正后自动重投递），无需额外操作
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/AntispamTT | Delays of several hours | Duplicate Delivery

symptom: EMT 中 Reverse ZAP 重投递时出现 STOREDRIVER DUPLICATEDELIVER 事件（AntispamTT），表示重投递被丢弃而非实际插入新副本
rootCause: 用户在 ZAP 执行前已删除原始邮件（ZAP 不对已删除邮件执行操作）；Reverse ZAP 尝试重投递时发现邮件已存在于收件箱，触发 DUPLICATEDELIVER（保留现有副本，丢弃重复）
solution: DUPLICATEDELIVER 属正常行为，无需额外操作；确认用户收件箱中仍有邮件副本即可；与其他收件人对比（DELIVER vs DUPLICATEDELIVER）可判断各自状态
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/AntispamTT | Delays of several hours | Duplicate Delivery

symptom: 外部批量发件人（P2 发件域非组织接受域）的邮件中嵌入图片未自动下载显示
rootCause: P2 发件地址不在收件人邮箱的安全发件人列表中；OWA/Outlook 默认不为非接受域发件人自动加载远程图片
solution: 将发件人地址添加到邮箱安全发件人列表（Outlook Safe Senders）；或通过 PowerShell/GPO 批量设置 Set-MailboxJunkEmailConfiguration；注意：接受域内的地址不建议加入安全发件人列表（防欺骗风险）
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/Automatic Image Download in Outlook

symptom: Classic Outlook 中，接受域（accepted domain）的第三方批量发送服务邮件图片未自动显示
rootCause: Classic Outlook 要求邮件通过内部认证（X-MS-Exchange-Organization-AuthAs: Internal），且 P2 发件地址必须能解析为组织内的收件人对象；若两个条件不满足则图片不自动下载
solution: 1) 检查邮件头中 AuthAs 是否为 Internal；2) 确认 P2 发件地址对应组织内可解析的对象（Get-Recipient <SMTPAddress>）；3) 或将图片加载域添加到 Trusted Sites Internet Zone（GPO 方式：User Configuration > Policies > Administrative Templates > Windows Components > Internet Explorer）；4) 注意：接受域地址避免加入安全发件人防止欺骗；5) 相关 feature request 见 ADO 303213
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/Automatic Image Download in Outlook

symptom: ZAP 对高可信度钓鱼邮件（High Confidence Phish）仍执行处置操作，即使 TABL 中已配置发件人允许（Sender Allow）规则
rootCause: 已知缺陷：ZAP 不遵守针对 High Confidence Phish 的 TABL 发件人允许规则（工程跟踪：Feature 6044388）
solution: 当前为已知问题，等待工程修复（Feature 6044388）；临时方案：使用 TABL URL Allow（当邮件中所有恶意 URL 均被 TABL URL 允许时，ZAP 会跳过该邮件）
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/TABL - FAQ

symptom: 管理员无法在 Tenant Allow/Block List (TABL) 中添加任何条目
rootCause: 管理员缺少管理 TABL 所需的 Exchange Online RBAC 或 URBAC 权限
solution: 1) 验证管理员已分配所需的 RBAC/URBAC 角色（参考 TABL 权限文档）；2) 确认 Exchange Online PowerShell 模块已安装；3) 使用正确的认证方式连接 Exchange Online PowerShell（Connect-ExchangeOnline）；4) 使用 Assist365 诊断 'Tenant Allow Block List (TABL) Search' 验证
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/TABL - FAQ

symptom: TABL 中已添加 URL 条目但未按预期执行阻止或允许
rootCause: URL 条目语法不正确，尤其是通配符（wildcards）使用方式不符合规范
solution: 检查 URL 条目的推荐语法，重点验证通配符用法；参考官方文档中的有效/无效 URL 条目场景：https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/defender-office-365/tenant-allow-block-list-urls-configure#url-entry-scenarios
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/TABL - FAQ

symptom: TABL 门户中欺骗发件人（Spoofed Sender）条目保存后消失/未持久化
rootCause: 租户中存在多个 HostedConnectionFilterPolicy；TABL 欺骗发件人条目仅从 isDefault=true 的策略读取，只支持一个策略实例
solution: 1) 修改第一个策略的 isDefault 值为 true；2) 删除多余的 HostedConnectionFilterPolicy；3) 重新添加欺骗发件人条目；注意：管理员无法通过 PowerShell 直接添加欺骗发件人（PowerShell 限制，仅用于排除 RBAC 或 cmdlet 问题时）
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/TABL - FAQ

symptom: TABL 欺骗发件人对（Spoofed Sender Pair）配置后仅在发送基础设施为子域时生效，使用顶级域时不匹配
rootCause: 当发送基础设施使用域名（而非 IP）时，该域名必须与连接 IP 的 PTR 记录匹配；直接使用子域名会导致匹配范围不准确
solution: 使用 PTR 组织域（PTR Organization Domain）作为发送基础设施值：先通过 `ping -a <IP>` 或 `Resolve-DnsName` 查询 PTR 记录，例如 PTR 解析为 smtp.inbound.contoso.com，则使用 contoso.com 作为基础设施域值
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/TABL - FAQ

symptom: Need to reopen a closed ADO/VSO issue AND simultaneously escalate it to CFL or PSI severity
rootCause: Reopened issues cannot have their severity directly changed to CFL or PSI on the existing item; CFL/PSI items must be created fresh through Assist
solution: Create a new item at CFL or PSI level via Assist (e.g. aka.ms/assist); reference the original ADO in the new item. No need to reopen the old item.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/[Deprecating] Working with MDO Pre-breach escalations (non-Spam Analyst)

symptom: ADO/VSO issue has an auto-close comment stating the issue must not be reopened
rootCause: Engineering has permanently closed the issue and added an explicit restriction against reopening; reopening would bypass engineering's decision
solution: Open a new ADO issue instead of reopening the existing one. Reference the original item in the new issue for context.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/[Deprecating] Working with MDO Pre-breach escalations (non-Spam Analyst)

symptom: MDO issue incorrectly routed to EEE ADO area paths — EEE triage is delayed or rejects the issue; or issue is a Spam Analyst (FP/FN), Attack Simulator, or Post-breach (non-pre-breach alert) case filed under EEE area
rootCause: EEEs only handle IP Engineering\Customer Escalations\Datacenter and IP Engineering\Customer Escalations\Org Security (excluding OSPREY). Spam Analyst, Attack Simulator, and Post-breach issues are out of EEE scope.
solution: Re-route: Spam Analyst FP/FN non-configuration issues → aka.ms/fpfn; MDO escalations (post-breach, Attack Simulator) → aka.ms/MDOEscalate; Pre-breach alerts may be handled by EEEs. Do not file under OSPREY area path for EEE cases.

symptom: 用户报告邮件延迟数小时才到达收件箱（延迟在 EOP 内部，非发送侧），或邮件先从收件箱消失后数小时重新出现；Received 头中出现 'MailboxResubmission by AntispamTT'；EMT（Extended Message Trace）中出现 AntispamTT 相关的 STOREDRIVER DELIVER 或 STOREDRIVER DUPLICATEDELIVER 事件；SAFETYNET RESUBMIT 事件出现在原始投递之后数小时
rootCause: ZAP（Zero-hour Auto Purge）对已投递的邮件触发了恶意检测（spam/phish/malware），将其隔离；EOP 随后判定该检测为 false-positive，reverse ZAP（AntispamTT）将邮件重新投递回收件箱。用户感知到的延迟即为原始投递与 reverse ZAP 重投之间的时间差。
solution: 1. 在 EMT 中查找 SAFETYNET RESUBMIT 事件和带 AntispamTT custom_data 的 STOREDRIVER 事件，确认时间线。2. 在管理员隔离区验证（邮件被重投后，隔离记录仍会保留）。3. 此行为属于预期设计（by design），向客户说明是 reverse ZAP false-positive 修正，无需额外操作。
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/AntispamTT | Delays of several hours | Duplicate Delivery

symptom: EMT 中 reverse ZAP 重投时出现 STOREDRIVER DUPLICATEDELIVER（而非 DELIVER）事件；邮件未被再次插入收件箱，或用户找不到重投的邮件
rootCause: 用户在 ZAP 执行隔离操作之前已将原始邮件从收件箱删除（ZAP 不对已删除邮件执行操作）；reverse ZAP 尝试重投时检测到邮件已存在（或已存副本），触发 DUPLICATEDELIVER — 邮件被丢弃，当前邮件状态保持不变。
solution: 向用户说明：DUPLICATEDELIVER 表示邮件仍在邮箱某处（当前副本被保留），并非丢失。如用户找不到邮件，建议检查已删除邮件 / 回收站。此行为属于预期设计。
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/AntispamTT | Delays of several hours | Duplicate Delivery

symptom: Customer reports a file falsely detected as malware by MDO/ATP in SharePoint Online (SPO) or OneDrive for Business (ODB); customer wants to unblock or re-enable access to the file
rootCause: MDO/ATP (Advanced Threat Protection) performs asynchronous scanning in SPO/ODB. False positive detection may occur. ATP/MDO detections are identified by malware names using underscores as separators (e.g., 'Malicious Payload', 'Trojan_MSI_SilentShellInjection')
solution: 1) Customer submits the file via the 'Email attachment' tab on the Defender Submissions page for MDO/ATP rescan. 2) If detection was <30 days ago: admin can release the file from MDO Quarantine. 3) If >30 days ago or anytime: customer re-uploads the file to the SPO/ODB site (scanning is async, no immediate re-detection). 4) If file is no longer in quarantine and customer cannot re-upload: engage SPO support for backend engineering unblock — MDO engineering CANNOT unblock directly as of 2025-06-19.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/[Do not use] Anti-malware in SPO and ODB [previous version]

symptom: Customer reports a file falsely detected as malware by the native MSAV scan engine in SharePoint Online or OneDrive for Business
rootCause: MSAV (Microsoft AV native scan engine) performs asynchronous scanning in SPO/ODB and may produce false positives. MSAV detections are identified by malware names containing '/' (e.g., 'DOS/EICAR_Test_File#', 'Win32/CryptInject!MSR'). MSAV is available to all customers regardless of license.
solution: Customer submits via the 'File' tab on the Defender Submissions page, or via WDSI (https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/wdsi/filesubmission) — sign in with corporate account for higher priority triaging. Both methods create a Windows Defender Security Intelligence submission for MSAV rescan. Use Assist 365 diagnostic 'Review ODSP and Teams Malware Verdicts' (provide SHA256 hash) to see scan results. After FP is confirmed and submission resolved, customer re-uploads file to the SPO/ODB site.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/[Do not use] Anti-malware in SPO and ODB [previous version]

symptom: File is blocked as malware in SPO/ODB but cannot be found in the MDO quarantine portal
rootCause: MDO Quarantine retains quarantined files for only 30 days after detection. After 30 days (or if manually deleted by admin), the quarantine entry disappears. Deletion from quarantine does NOT remove the block on the actual file in SPO/ODB — the file remains blocked in the document library.
solution: 1) Check quarantine with the correct date range (within 30 days of detection date). 2) To find original detection date: use UAL with Search-UnifiedAuditLog -Operations FileMalwareDetected. 3) If <30 days: release from MDO quarantine via admin portal. 4) If file is no longer in quarantine: customer can re-upload the file to the SPO/ODB site. 5) If customer cannot re-upload and file is still blocked: engineering unblock via SPO support team is required (MDO engineering cannot unblock).
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/[Do not use] Anti-malware in SPO and ODB [previous version]

symptom: Engineer cannot determine whether a malware detection in SPO/ODB was made by MSAV or ATP/MDO, causing uncertainty about which submission path to use or which team to escalate to
rootCause: SPO/ODB has two separate anti-malware engines: MSAV (native, all customers) and ATP/MDO (E5 or add-on). They use different malware naming conventions that can be used to distinguish detections.
solution: Identify by malware name convention: MSAV: name contains '/' (e.g., 'DOS/EICAR_Test_File#', 'X97M/Slacker.gen!A', 'Win32/CryptInject!MSR'). ATP/MDO: name uses '_' as separator OR is called 'Malicious Payload' (e.g., 'Malicious Payload', 'Trojan_MSI_SilentShellInjection', 'Trojan_PDF_LinkedUrlCookie_A'). Use Assist 365 diagnostic 'Review ODSP and Teams Malware Verdicts' with SHA256 file hash for a definitive verdict and unblock guidance. Advanced hunting table FileMaliciousContentInfo also available for MDO detections.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Drafts/[Do not use] Anti-malware in SPO and ODB [previous version]

symptom: DKIM fails with 'No Key For Signature' even though DKIM DNS records exist and are correctly configured, or SPF/DMARC fails with 'temperror' despite DNS records being present
rootCause: EOP has an internal DNS lookup timeout of 500ms. If the authoritative DNS server hosting SPF/DKIM/DMARC records responds slower than 500ms, EOP fails the lookup even if a response eventually arrives. The issue is slow DNS server response time, not missing records.
solution: 1) Use TSG 'Auth timeouts and failures due to timeouts' (https://dev.azure.com/ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/_wiki/wikis/Messaging Protection Wiki/11956) to gather details and assess prevalence. 2) Advise customer to investigate latency on the NS hosting their records (use external tools like dig/nslookup to measure response times). 3) Customer should consider using a faster DNS provider for their SPF/DKIM/DMARC records. 4) To confirm root cause via backend hygiene logs: escalate to MDO Escalations team.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Known Issues

symptom: Administrator unable to modify or save spam filter (hosted content filter) policies. UI shows error: 'The operation could not be completed. Please try again later. Microsoft has been made aware of this issue.' HAR/Fiddler shows: 'XJ is not a valid ISO 3166-1 region code' or 'Failed to fetch region data'
rootCause: The policy's RegionBlockList contains country codes that are either no longer valid ISO 3166-1 codes (XJ=Jan Mayen, XE=Sint Eustatius, XS=Saba) or are valid ISO codes not yet supported by the UI (SS=South Sudan, EH=Western Sahara). Saving the policy fails when any of these codes are present.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Known Issues

symptom: Spoofed emails are delivered as false negatives with compauth result code 451 when the customer's MX record does not point to EOP; spoof intelligence is not blocking these messages
rootCause: Two possible causes: 1) MX points to a 3rd-party filtering service and inbound connectors are not restricted — anyone can bypass the filter and send directly to M365 (Direct Send), resulting in compauth=451 because DMARC/SPF cannot be verified for these direct-delivered messages. 2) Internal DNS timeout when EOP looks up the recipient domain MX record, causing a transient authentication failure.
solution: Cause 1: Restrict inbound connectors so only the 3rd-party filtering service can deliver directly to M365. Reference: 'What is Direct Send and how to secure it' (techcommunity blog). Additional override list option available — see wiki 'Understanding Direct Send Spoofing (CompAuth = 451 + 905)'. Cause 2: Ensure customer's DNS TTLs are set to at least 3600, then escalate to MDO Escalations team to add tenant to the retry list.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Known Issues

symptom: Customer cannot enable DKIM signing configuration when their tenant's initial domain is *.onmicrosoft.de; DKIM configuration setup fails
rootCause: By design: EOP's DKIM configuration only supports initial domains with the *.onmicrosoft.com suffix. The *.onmicrosoft.de domain suffix is not supported for DKIM key setup. Confirmed by Engineering in IcM 613481576.
solution: Self-serve workaround: Customer must first change their initial domain from *.onmicrosoft.de to *.onmicrosoft.com in the Microsoft 365 admin center. Then remove any existing DKIM configuration and recreate it under the new initial domain. Note: The 'Validate DKIM Signing Configuration' diagnostic was updated in May 2025 to detect and surface this scenario.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Known Issues

symptom: Customer cannot add trusted senders (individual email addresses) for impersonation protection in the Defender Standard Preset Security Policy through the UI; only trusted domains can be added via the portal
rootCause: Known bug in Preset Security Policy UI: the trusted senders/domains section does not accept individual sender email addresses. Bug 6646002. IcM workaround documented in Incident-699374069.

symptom: Third-party phishing simulation emails are filtered by EOP (as malware or high confidence phishing) instead of being delivered unfiltered, even after configuring Advanced Delivery policy
rootCause: The sending domain (P1Sender/MAILFROM domain or DKIM-signing domain) or sending IP configured in the Advanced Delivery phishing simulation policy does not match the actual message. Both the correct sending domain AND the sending IP must be present in the policy for bypass to apply.
solution: 1. Collect Authentication-Results header from a sample message to identify sending IP, P1Sender domain (smtp.mailfrom), and DKIM domain (header.d). 2. Run Get-ExoPhishSimOverrideRule and compare SenderIpRanges and Domains. 3. Ensure the actual sending IP is in SenderIpRanges AND either the P1Sender domain or DKIM-signing domain is in Domains. 4. If DKIM domain is configured, DKIM must also pass (dkim=pass). 5. Note: since MC545900, configuring simulation URLs is no longer required for email-based simulations.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Advanced Delivery

symptom: Advanced Delivery policy is configured with DKIM-signing domain but phishing simulation emails are still filtered by EOP instead of bypassed
rootCause: DKIM signing domain is configured in Advanced Delivery, but the message's DKIM signature fails validation (dkim=fail or dkim=none in Authentication-Results header). Advanced Delivery only bypasses filtering when the configured DKIM domain is present AND DKIM validation passes.
solution: Check Authentication-Results header: if dkim=fail or dkim=none, DKIM is not signing or passing. Fix options: (1) Fix DKIM signing on the sending system so dkim=pass appears in Authentication-Results, OR (2) Add the P1Sender/MAILFROM domain to Advanced Delivery Domains list instead of relying on DKIM. When escalating to Engineering, include full message headers and timeline of configuration changes.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Advanced Delivery

symptom: Admin notices unexpected reduction in volume of emails shown as blocked by transport rules ('Rule Messages' / 'Rule Block') in the Mailflow status report
rootCause: Microsoft updated the Mailflow status report counting behavior: previously counted all emails that matched any transport rule (regardless of action); now only counts emails that matched a transport rule AND were quarantined by that rule. This is a reporting accuracy improvement, not a filtering regression.
solution: Inform the customer this is expected behavior per MC698146. The report now accurately reflects only emails quarantined by transport rules. No action is needed; there is no loss of functionality or security. The reduction in count is expected.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Blocked transport rule count volume in Mailflow status report

symptom: Third-party phishing simulation messages are being blocked or detonated by MDO; customer expects them to be delivered unfiltered as part of a simulation exercise
rootCause: Third-party simulation tools are outside the scope of Microsoft Attack Simulation and Training (AST). The messages are subject to EOP/MDO filtering unless explicitly bypassed via Advanced Delivery policy.
solution: Redirect to Advanced Delivery policy configuration. This is not an AST issue. Customer must configure the 3rd party simulation vendor's sending domain (P1Sender or DKIM domain) and sending IP in the Advanced Delivery > Third-party phishing simulations section of Microsoft Defender portal. Refer to the Advanced Delivery troubleshooting guide for detailed steps.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Attack Simulation Training FAQ

symptom: Microsoft Attack Simulation Training simulation emails, training reminders, notification messages, or QR code simulations not received by targeted users
rootCause: Various potential causes depending on scenario (delivery pipeline, backend simulation engine, tenant configuration, RBAC, payload issues). Specific root cause requires minimum log collection and AST product team escalation.
solution: Collect minimum required logs before escalation per scenario: (1) Email/reminder/notification not received: TenantId + simulationId/simulationName + affected user email addresses. (2) Backend issues: TenantId + simulationId/simulationName. (3) QR code delay/failure: TenantId + simulationId + network capture. (4) Payload issues (HTML, image, phishing link, URL blocked): TenantId + simulationId + screenshot/network capture. (5) RBAC issues: TenantId + simulationId + Entra/Azure Portal configs. Submit with all required logs for AST team escalation.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Attack Simulation Training FAQ

rootCause: Gmail blocking due to poor domain/IP reputation. Common causes: missing or failing SPF/DKIM/DMARC authentication, domain not verified with Gmail Postmaster Tools, or high spam rate from sending domain/IP.
solution: 1) Verify SPF/DKIM/DMARC all configured and passing per https://techcommunity.microsoft.com/t5/exchange-team-blog/authenticate-outbound-email-to-improve-deliverability/ba-p/3947623. 2) For domain errors: verify domain with Gmail Postmaster Tools (postmaster.google.com), check Domain Reputation/Spam Rate/Authentication pages. 3) For IP errors: review Google Sending Guidelines at https://support.google.com/mail/answer/81126. 4) Before escalating to PG, confirm authentication passes and reputation is good - include screenshots.

symptom: GoDaddy tenant blocked with 5.7.705 Tenant Exceeded Threshold error
rootCause: Tenant exceeded outbound spam threshold due to compromised account. GoDaddy cases use shared tenant UnityForDogBarking.co (0489dd82-284e-48f5-8744-f815bd65a769).
solution: 1) ALL correspondence via EMAIL only - DO NOT CALL. 2) Run diagnostics with Add Alternate Tenant using PartnerEndCustomer Tenant ID. 3) Run EOP Domain Health (manual blocklist? throttled events? connectors deleted?), EOP User Health (MFA enabled?), Display Mail Flow Overview (Security Defaults?). 4) If Manual Blocklist: escalate per guidance. 5) If only ThrottledTenant: run Tenant Exceeded Threshold diagnostic to release. 6) If diagnostic removes block, send confirmation and close - no need to wait for GoDaddy response. 7) For EU tenants: use EU Assist Add Alternate Tenant and editable Tenant ID in Escalation tab.

symptom: SPF/DKIM/DMARC returns temperror/timeout intermittently for inbound email. Auth-Results header shows dkim=timeout or dmarc=temperror or spf=temperror.
rootCause: DNS TTL values for SPF/DKIM/DMARC records (including sub-entries like nested SPF includes, DKIM CNAME-to-TXT chains) are below 3600 seconds, causing frequent cache expiry and DNS lookup delays exceeding the MDO 500ms timeout threshold.
solution: 1) Check all DNS TTLs (top-level and sub-entries) using digwebinterface.com with Authoritative server option. 2) Increase all TTLs to >=3600 seconds. 3) For SPF, check nested includes with Vamsoft SPF Policy Tester. 4) For DKIM, check selector CNAME and TXT record TTLs. 5) If issue persists after TTL fix and is quantifiable, escalate to MDO Escalations -- engineering may enable a 1000ms retry timeout. Do NOT offer the extended timeout directly to customers.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Email Authentication/TSG: Auth timeouts, and failures due to timeouts

symptom: SPF shows softfail/hardfail (not temperror) but the sender's SPF record appears correct. Sub-entry timeouts in deeply nested SPF includes cause the top-level check to produce a fail result instead of temperror.
rootCause: Complex SPF records with many nested includes (e.g., 6+ levels) cause cumulative DNS lookup time to exceed 500ms. When a sub-entry times out, SPF returns fail (determined by ~all/-all mechanism), not temperror -- making timeout hard to identify as root cause.
solution: 1) Use Vamsoft SPF Policy Tester to expand and check total query/response time. 2) Reduce nested include complexity. 3) Increase sub-entry TTLs to >=3600s. 4) For macros in SPF, expand using RFC 4408 section 7.2 or Vamsoft advanced option. 5) Quantify SPF fail rates with Advanced Hunting to determine if escalation is warranted.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Email Authentication/TSG: Auth timeouts, and failures due to timeouts

symptom: Auto-forwarded emails from tenant A to tenant B fail DMARC and are marked as spoof. Auth-Results shows dmarc=fail, SPF domain is rewritten by SRS to forwarding tenant domain (P1 != P2), DKIM for original sender fails with body hash did not verify.
rootCause: SRS rewrites P1 (MailFrom) sender with forwarding tenant domain on forward, breaking SPF alignment with P2 (From). If a 3rd-party email service (MX for forwarding tenant) modifies message body/headers, DKIM also breaks. Without aligned SPF or DKIM, DMARC fails.
solution: 1) Primary: ensure original sender DKIM-signs with P2 (From) domain AND prevent message modification in transit. 2) If not possible: add TABL spoof allow -- * as spoofed sender, forwarding tenant onmicrosoft.com as sending infrastructure (NOT outlook.com). 3) If 3rd-party supports ARC, add as trusted ARC sealer. Check Authentication-Results-Original header to determine where DKIM breaks. Note: TABL and ARC sealers have limitations in 21V.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Email Authentication/TSG: Email authentication in a forwarding scenario

symptom: SPF fails for inbound email when customer MX record does not point to EOP (on-premises/3rd-party filtering in front of EOP). Auth-Results shows spf=fail with the connecting IP belonging to the customer's intermediate server.
rootCause: SPF check evaluates the connecting IP from the on-premises/3rd-party server, not the original sender's IP. The intermediate server IP is not in the sender's SPF record.
solution: 1) Enable Enhanced Filtering for Connectors (skip listing) to evaluate SPF against the original sender IP. 2) Lock down Exchange Online to accept mail only from the 3rd-party (Step 4 best practices). 3) Do NOT bypass spam filtering with transport rules. 4) Honor DMARC will apply when Enhanced Filtering is enabled even if MX does not point to EOP.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Email Authentication/Troubleshooting Email Authentication Failures

symptom: DKIM fails with 'body hash did not verify' (dkim=fail body hash fail). Message body was modified after DKIM signature was applied.
rootCause: 3rd-party or on-premises MX modified the message body after DKIM signing -- common causes: URL/link wrapping, external sender warning banners, message signatures/footers.
solution: 1) Primary: stop body modifications before DKIM verification at EOP. 2) Alternative: configure trusted ARC sealers if 3rd-party supports ARC (not available in 21V/Mooncake). 3) For EOP-to-3rd-party-to-EOP routing: use ARC or TABL spoof allow for 3rd-party IPs. 4) New Enhanced Filtering improvement: if MX not at EOP + EF enabled + DKIM domain aligns with From but body hash fails, CompAuth set to 'none' instead of 'fail'.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Email Authentication/Troubleshooting Email Authentication Failures

symptom: DKIM fails with 'signature did not verify' (dkim=fail signature did not verify). A header included in the DKIM signature was modified after signing.
rootCause: An intermediate server modified one of the headers listed in the DKIM-Signature h= value. Subject header is the most common culprit (e.g., prepending [EXTERNAL]).
solution: 1) Check h= value in DKIM-Signature header to identify signed headers. 2) Stop header modifications by intermediate servers. 3) Alternative: configure trusted ARC sealers if supported (not available in 21V). 4) For auto-forwarded emails with DKIM domain not aligned with P2 From domain, enable DKIM signing for the vanity domain (not just onmicrosoft.com).
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Email Authentication/Troubleshooting Email Authentication Failures

symptom: SPF returns permerror for sender domain. Email authentication fails permanently, not intermittently.
rootCause: SPF record has syntax errors OR exceeds the RFC 7208 limit of 10 DNS lookups across all nested includes/redirects/a/mx/ptr/exists mechanisms.
solution: 1) Validate SPF syntax with MXToolbox or DMARCIAN. 2) Count total DNS lookups across all nested levels (include, redirect, a, mx, ptr, exists count). 3) To reduce: remove stale entries, replace includes with ip4/ip6, use DKIM for some 3rd-party senders, use transport rules for internal-only senders. 4) If syntax correct and lookups <=10 but permerror persists only in O365 (passes at Gmail/Yahoo), escalate to MDO Escalations with message sample + 3rd-party test results.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Email Authentication/Troubleshooting Email Authentication Failures

symptom: Emails from M365 tenant blocked/rejected by Gmail. Errors: '550 5.7.1 message detected as spam due to low reputation' or '421 4.7.28 unusual rate of unsolicited mail, temporarily rate limited'.
rootCause: Email authentication (SPF/DKIM/DMARC) not properly configured, or sending domain/IP has low reputation with Gmail. For high-volume senders (5000+/day), Gmail requires DKIM+DMARC per April 2025 requirements.
solution: 1) Configure SPF, DKIM, DMARC per https://aka.ms/emailauth. 2) Verify domain with Gmail Postmaster Tools (postmaster.google.com). 3) Check domain reputation and spam rate. 4) For domain errors: Google TXT record verification. 5) For IP errors: review Google Bulk Email Sender Guidelines. 6) If all configured correctly, redirect customer to consumer support. 7) Before engineering escalation, include Postmaster Tools reputation screenshots.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Gmail Deliverability Issues

symptom: GoDaddy partner tenant outbound email blocked with '550 5.7.705 Tenant Exceeded Threshold'. All GoDaddy cases logged under UnityForDogBarking.co tenant (0489dd82-284e-48f5-8744-f815bd65a769).
rootCause: Tenant blocked due to detected spam/compromise activity -- either on throttle list or manual blocklist. GoDaddy is CSP partner; requires special diagnostic handling using Add Alternate Tenant with PartnerEndCustomer Tenant ID.
solution: 1) Confirm GoDaddy mitigated: password reset + MFA/Security Defaults on affected account. 2) Run EOP Domain Health (Add Alternate Tenant with PartnerEndCustomer ID). 3) Run EOP User Health to verify MFA. 4) If throttled only: run Tenant Exceeded Threshold diagnostic to release -- if successful, email confirmation and close. 5) If on manual blocklist: escalate via Partner-Tenant Escalations. CRITICAL: EMAIL only, never call, never contact end customer directly.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/GoDaddy Special Handling 5 7 705

symptom: Inbound emails show SPF=TEMPERROR (DNS Timeout) or DKIM=FAIL (no key for signature) in Authentication-Results header
rootCause: Sending domain SPF or DKIM DNS records have TTL lower than 3600 seconds, causing DNS lookup timeouts in EOP
solution: Verify TTL of SPF (TXT) and DKIM (CNAME) records using Resolve-DnsName; ensure TTL is set to 3600. If customer controls DNS, set TTL to 3600. For third-party senders with low TTL, note in escalation.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Handling SPF and DKIM Timeout Issues

symptom: SPF=TEMPERROR for domains using macro-based SPF records or nested/flattened SPF records
rootCause: include:spf.protection.outlook.com is missing from the main (top-level) SPF TXT record, causing EOP DNS resolution issues with complex SPF configurations
solution: Add include:spf.protection.outlook.com at the beginning of the main SPF TXT record. For nested/flattened records, ensure it is in the main TXT record, not a subrecord.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Handling SPF and DKIM Timeout Issues

symptom: SPF/DKIM DNS timeout failure rate exceeds 0.1-0.2% for specific sending domains even after TTL and SPF record corrections
rootCause: Sending domain is not on the AntispamRetrySpfWithExtendedTimeouts retry list in ECS (Experimentation Configuration Service)
solution: Check ECS configuration (Substrate/EOP_Antispam config 1423964, ExtendedtimeoutV3 column) for the domain/GUID. If not present and failures persist, escalate to MDO Engineering with: Advanced Hunting results, domain list, headers, DNS output, retry list status.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Handling SPF and DKIM Timeout Issues

symptom: Legitimate email incorrectly classified as High Confidence Phishing (HPHISH) and quarantined by Secure by Default (SBD)
rootCause: EOP filtering engine misclassified the message as HPHISH; SBD always quarantines HPHISH regardless of admin overrides (allowed sender lists, Safe Senders, IP Allow Lists, ETRs)
solution: 1) Run MDO Message Explorer diagnostic in Assist 365 to identify HPHISH classification reason. 2) Submit sample via Microsoft 365 Defender > Submissions > Allow messages like this to create temporary Tenant Allow/Block List entry. 3) Fix email authentication (SPF/DKIM/DMARC) if contributing to verdict.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/In-depth defense with dual-use scenario

symptom: Missed detections, false positives, or policy gaps in dual-use deployment where MX points to third-party SEG before Microsoft 365
rootCause: Enhanced Filtering for Connectors (EFC) is not configured or incorrectly configured, causing EOP to lose original sender IP and authentication results when mail passes through third-party gateway
solution: Configure Enhanced Filtering for Connectors to preserve original sender IP and authentication. Run Defender for Office 365 Threat Policy Health Check diagnostic (https://aka.ms/thc) to verify EFC coverage.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/In-depth defense with dual-use scenario

symptom: Microsoft Entra ID Licenses page shows 0/0 MDO Plan 1 or Plan 2 licenses assigned, admin concerned about licensing gap
rootCause: MDO is a tenant-level Microsoft 365 service plan, NOT an Entra ID-assignable product. Per-user Entra assignment is not required; protections apply tenant-wide once subscription is active.
solution: Validate MDO coverage in Microsoft 365 Admin Center > Billing > Your Products (not Entra ID). Verify Purchase Quantity covers all protected users. Quick checks: Safe Links/Anti-phishing policies editable = Plan 1 active; Threat Explorer available = Plan 2 active.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/MDO Licensing Plans Features & Tenant Level Behaviour

symptom: Defender portal permissions not effective after activating role via PIM for Groups (indirect JIT); user cannot access expected features for up to 2 hours
rootCause: Defender RBAC and Unified RBAC (URBAC) roles cannot be assigned directly through PIM. When using PIM for Groups as workaround, Defender may take up to 2 hours to detect new group members.
solution: Prefer PIM for Entra Roles (direct activation, ~10-15 min propagation) over PIM for Groups. If group-scoped RBAC required, consider enrolling in preview feature 4581709 (reduces detection to ~5-15 min for legacy Defender RBAC). URBAC does not benefit from this preview.

symptom: Email from blocked sender delivered to Junk folder instead of being quarantined, or vice versa. X-Forefront-Antispam-Report shows SFV:BLK and SCL:6.
rootCause: When a sender is on a user Blocked Senders list, EOP assigns SCL:6 (SFV:BLK). The actual action (Move to Junk vs Quarantine) depends on the anti-spam policy spam action setting, not the block list itself.
solution: Check the anti-spam policy spam action configuration. If quarantine is desired for blocked senders, set the spam action to Quarantine message. Verify verdict with X-Forefront-Antispam-Report header (SFV:BLK). Use Spam Verdict Reason diagnostic to confirm.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Mailbox Allowed Blocked Senders and Limits

symptom: Domains added to user Allowed Senders list in junk email configuration are not honored; emails from allowed domains are still being filtered by EOP.
rootCause: Domain-based entries in a user Allowed Senders list are NOT honored by Exchange Online. This is by design. Domain entries are evaluated only at the mailbox level and cannot be synchronized to Microsoft Entra ID. Allowing entire domains would introduce security risk via domain spoofing.
solution: Add specific email addresses instead of domains to the Allowed Senders list. For organization-wide domain allows, use anti-spam policy allowed domains or Exchange transport rules. Verify with Get-MailboxJunkEmailConfiguration cmdlet.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Mailbox Allowed Blocked Senders and Limits

symptom: Allowed/blocked senders list not working as expected with Quarantine action. Safe sender messages getting quarantined unexpectedly, or blocked sender messages reaching Junk folder instead of quarantine.
rootCause: Junk mail hash stored in AAD has size limits: 3072 entries for allowed senders, 2048 for allowed recipients, 500 for blocked senders. Exceeding these limits causes the hash to not stay updated, leading to stale allow/block data during transport filtering. Shared Mailboxes are especially affected as hashes do not auto-sync.
solution: Reduce entries to stay within hash limits (3072 allowed, 2048 recipients, 500 blocked). If ContactsTrusted is enabled, contacts count toward the hash - consider disabling it. For Shared Mailboxes exceeding limits, use non-customer-facing diagnostic to force hash sync. Avoid Quarantine action if lists exceed hash limits.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Mailbox Allowed Blocked Senders and Limits

symptom: Outlook client shows error 'You are over the Junk E-mail list limit' when trying to modify blocked or allowed senders list.
rootCause: Outlook client has a hard limit of 510KB on the junk mail rule size. Large allowed/blocked sender lists cause the rule to exceed this limit. This limit is owned by the Outlook team and cannot be increased.
solution: Manage junk email lists via PowerShell (Set-MailboxJunkEmailConfiguration) instead of the Outlook client. Reduce the number of entries in allowed/blocked senders lists to stay under the 510KB rule size limit.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Mailbox Allowed Blocked Senders and Limits

symptom: Email blocked or quarantined as malware by Common Attachment Filter detecting .COM file type, but the actual attachment is not a .COM file. A nested/attached message has a subject line ending in .COM.
rootCause: Security hardening change by Microsoft. Defender for Office 365 applies stricter filetype evaluation to prevent bypass exploits. Subject lines ending in .COM in attached/nested messages now trigger .COM file type detection in Common Attachment Filter.
solution: This is by design. Review and release blocked items through existing quarantine workflows if appropriate. No customer remediation action required. Admin notifications will show the detected file type.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Malware TSG

symptom: NDR received when sending email with attachment. Status: 550 5.0.350 One or more of the attachments in your email is of a file type that is NOT allowed. Attachment contains nested/embedded attachments with depth >= 20.
rootCause: Common Attachment Filter rejects emails when an attachment contains nested attachments with a depth of 20 or more levels, triggering policy violation NDR.
solution: Reduce the nesting depth of attachments to below 20 levels. If business justification exists, file an escalation with the antispam team. Check FileTypeAction setting: Reject sends NDR, Quarantine silently quarantines.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Malware TSG

symptom: Customer receives 400 or 403 error when trying to access https://security.microsoft.com/alerts page in Microsoft Defender portal.
rootCause: The /alerts page requires Defender for Office 365 Plan 2 (or E5) license, or another security product with E5/P2 license (e.g., MDE P2). Customers with P1/E3 license do not have access to this URL.
solution: For P1/E3 customers, use https://security.microsoft.com/viewAlerts instead. If customer has P2/E5 and still gets 400/403, collect F12/HAR traces (must include TenantContext API call and Alerts page error request) plus PSR file, then open ICM to EOP/MDOCustomerEscalationsEEE (TIER 1).
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Post Breach/Alerts/Unable to access alerts page in Defender 400 or 403 error

symptom: Detonation tree is not showing data on the Email Entity page in Microsoft Defender portal for a URL or attachment analysis.
rootCause: Detonation tree is only created when dynamic analysis actually occurs (Safe Links URL rewriting/click-time evaluation, active sandbox detonation). It is NOT created for: static-only verdicts (reputation/blocklists/heuristics), no Safe Links involvement (disabled or Do Not Rewrite list), no user interaction (URL never clicked), analysis skipped/short-circuited, or expired detonation artifacts.
solution: Verify if the URL/file went through dynamic analysis: check if Safe Links was enabled and if URL was clicked. For static verdicts or skipped analysis, no detonation tree is expected (by design). Check API endpoint /di/Find/OneCyberDetonationData for raw data if available.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Post Breach/Email Entity/Detonation tree is not showing data

symptom: Take Action button/option is not available or missing in Advanced Hunting for email remediation actions in Microsoft Defender portal.
rootCause: Missing URBAC permissions. The Take Action option requires specific Defender XDR URBAC permissions: Security operations > Security data > Security data basic (read) > Response (manage) and Email & collaboration advanced actions (manage). Also requires Defender for Office 365 Plan 2 license.
solution: Assign the required URBAC permissions: Security data basic (read), Response (manage), and Email & collaboration advanced actions (manage). Ensure user has MDO P2 license. If permissions are correctly assigned and Take Action still missing, verify URBAC is activated for MDO workload.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Post Breach/MDO Advanced hunting

symptom: Outbound forwarded/relayed email from M365 lands in recipient Junk or is rejected
rootCause: Messages sent via Relay Pool: sender not in accepted domain, SPF fails, DKIM not configured. Relay Pool IPs (40.95.0.0/16) not in SPF records.
solution: Ensure forwarded messages meet ONE: (1) sender in accepted domain, (2) SPF passes on inbound, (3) DKIM passes. For third-party MX, enable Enhanced Filtering.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Relay Pools

symptom: SPF fails for forwarded messages through M365, DMARC fails at recipient, rejected by DMARC policy
rootCause: SRS not applied for Relay Pool. P1 From not modified, causing SPF fail at recipient.
solution: Ensure SPF or DKIM passes on inbound so SRS applied and message sent via Regular Pool. Use Enhanced Filtering if MX to third-party.

symptom: Protected user personal email to work account flagged as User Impersonation (CAT:UIMP)

solution: Add personal address to Safe Senders list. Mailbox Intelligence learns relationship over time.

symptom: Legitimate external email flagged as spoofing (CAT:SPOOF) due to auth failure from known sender

symptom: Cannot add IPv6 to Connection Filter IP Allow/Block List

symptom: IP on Allow List but still blocked by Spamhaus
rootCause: IP Allow List does NOT override Spamhaus. Neither Allow List nor Transport Rules help.
solution: Customer must delist from Spamhaus directly. For critical enterprise, temporary Manual List override possible.

rootCause: Too much spam/bulk from organization.

symptom: NDR 4.7.500 - Server busy, gray-listed
rootCause: Source IP changed sending patterns drastically. Probation period.

symptom: NDR 5.7.511 - Sender IP banned, self-delist NOT available
rootCause: Microsoft requires investigation. Delist portal unavailable for 5.7.511.

symptom: Enhanced Filtering (skip listing) defaults to connecting IP instead of skipping it; all previous IPs in received headers are private (10.x/172.16.x/192.168.x)
rootCause: When every previous IP in the received headers is a private IP, EF cannot determine the original sending IP and defaults to the last valid (connecting) IP. Some on-prem/external MX servers privatize their IPs in headers.
solution: Fix on-prem/external MX side to include real public IPs in received headers, or use a solution other than skip listing.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Enhanced Filtering for Connectors

symptom: Enhanced Filtering EFUsers is populated but skip listing not working for hybrid user
rootCause: EFUsers applies skip listing only to listed addresses (not users). Secondary proxies must be explicitly listed. In hybrid setups, the coexistence address (user@contoso.mail.onmicrosoft.com) must be specified.
solution: Add the targetAddress of the MailUser object (e.g. user@contoso.mail.onmicrosoft.com) to EFUsers. If EFUsers is blank, skip listing applies to everyone.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Enhanced Filtering for Connectors

symptom: Enhanced Filtering skip listing not applied - no skip listing headers present in message
rootCause: Message was attributed to a different inbound connector that does not have Enhanced Filtering configured. Many tenants have multiple inbound connectors.
solution: Verify which inbound connector was applied to the message using the Message Protection Policy Overview diagnostic. Ensure the correct connector has Enhanced Filtering settings.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Enhanced Filtering for Connectors

symptom: Set-MailboxJunkEmailConfiguration fails with "Junk e-mail validation error" when adding to TrustedSendersAndDomains
rootCause: The number of Trusted Senders being synchronized to the hash exceeds the supported limit, typically caused by ContactsTrusted enabled and/or excessive Trusted Senders entries.
solution: Disable ContactsTrusted and/or reduce the number of configured Trusted Senders to bring total within supported limit.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Junk e mail validation error message in an Exchange Online environment

symptom: Set-MailboxJunkEmailConfiguration fails with "Property validation failed: TrustedSendersAndDomains"
rootCause: Existing entries in TrustedSendersAndDomains contain wildcards or valid internal mailbox/distribution group addresses which are not allowed.
solution: Remove invalid entries (wildcards, internal mailbox/DG addresses) from the Safe Sender list in Outlook, then retry the cmdlet.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Junk e mail validation error message in an Exchange Online environment

symptom: Junk email filter settings in Outlook revert to default after refresh; Set-MailboxJunkEmailConfiguration returns ErrorCode=2 Property validation failed
rootCause: BlockedSendersAndDomains list has reached the maximum 1024 entries.
solution: Remove invalid/unnecessary entries and ensure the BlockedSendersAndDomains list has fewer than 1024 entries.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Junk e mail validation error message in an Exchange Online environment

symptom: Blocked/Safe sender domain added without @ symbol in Outlook not reflected in Get-MailboxJunkEmailConfiguration or OWA
rootCause: Adding domain without @ only updates property 6106001f (JunkBlockedDomainSuffixes) which works only on Outlook client when open. PR_EXTENDED_RULE_MSG_CONDITION (needed for server-side + OWA) only updates with @ prefix.
solution: Always add domains using @ prefix (e.g. @contoso.com) in Outlook client to ensure server-side enforcement.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Junk e mail validation error message in an Exchange Online environment

symptom: Self-domain spoofing emails delivered to inbox via Direct Send when MX does not point to Microsoft 365; CompAuth=none reason=451 or 905; AuthAs=Anonymous
rootCause: Direct Send allows any Internet device on port 25 to send to M365 mailboxes without authentication. When MX does not point to M365, DMARC policies are not honored and CompAuth checks can be bypassed.
solution: Option 1: Lock down M365 to only accept mail from third-party service (connector lockdown). Option 2: Enable Reject Direct Send feature. Option 3: Escalate to MDO Escalations Tier 1 for manual internal list. Temp workaround: transport rule to quarantine messages failing DMARC from accepted domains.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Understanding Direct Send Spoofing (CompAuth = 451 + 905)

symptom: Cannot obtain outbound spam samples with proper X-Forefront-Antispam-Report headers for FP investigation; Sent Items lack filtering headers
rootCause: Messages in Sent Items have not been subject to filtering and therefore lack the X-Forefront-Antispam-Report header values needed for diagnosis.
solution: Enable "Send a copy of outbound messages that exceed these limits to these users and groups" in the Default Anti-spam outbound policy (not custom policies). Specify a valid BCC recipient address.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Use outbound spam BCC alerting to collect spam samples

symptom: Email delivered to Junk folder reported as FP, but X-Forefront-Antispam-Report shows SFV:NSPM and CAT:None (EOP did not classify as spam)
rootCause: Inbox rules (user custom filters) moved the message to Junk. X-Microsoft-Antispam-Mailbox-Delivery shows ucf:1, dest:J, OFR:CustomRules.
solution: Check X-Microsoft-Antispam-Mailbox-Delivery header for ucf:1/OFR:CustomRules. Not a true FP - advise customer to review inbox rules. No escalation to Analysts needed.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting False Positives and False Negatives

symptom: Legitimate emails junked with spam verdict "detected as a Mail Bomb attack" (Mail Bombing detection false positive)
rootCause: New Mail Bombing detection technology auto-tags subscription-type emails matching mail bombing patterns, catching legitimate subscriptions.
solution: Use TABL Sender Allow for specific senders. To opt out entirely, escalate to Exchange Online/Analysts (FPFN) with diagnostic results.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Mail Bombing

symptom: Quarantined message cannot be found by end user or admin in quarantine portal
rootCause: Most common causes: (1) message expired past retention period (default 15 days), (2) message from Blocked Sender filtered out by default, (3) message deleted from quarantine.
solution: Check retention period in anti-spam policy vs message age. For blocked sender: use -IncludeMessagesFromBlockedSenderAddress parameter. For deletion: search Unified Audit Log for RecordType Quarantine + Operation QuarantineDeleteMessage.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Quarantine Operations Policies and Notifications

symptom: Quarantine notifications not being sent to end users at expected frequency or at all
rootCause: Quarantine notifications controlled by per-policy ESNEnabled (must be True) and Global Quarantine Policy frequency. Settings stamped at delivery time - changing policy after quarantine won't retroactively apply.
solution: Verify: (1) quarantine policy has ESNEnabled=True, (2) check global frequency via Get-QuarantinePolicy -QuarantinePolicyType GlobalQuarantinePolicy, (3) trace quarantine@messaging.microsoft.com delivery.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Troubleshooting Quarantine Operations Policies and Notifications

symptom: User blocked for exceeding recipient rate limit unexpectedly; message trace shows fewer messages than reported
rootCause: Rate limits count against authenticated user, not From address. SendAs/SendOnBehalf causes authenticating user tally to include messages sent as other mailboxes. Outbound spam policy applied is based on From address causing policy mismatch.
solution: Identify SendAs/SendOnBehalf via audit logs or Get-RecipientPermission. Align outbound spam policy for all accounts the user sends as. Use MDOThreatPolicyChecker to detect mismatches.
sourceRef: ASIM-Security/Messaging Protection/Messaging Protection Wiki:/Troubleshooting Guides/Understanding and Troubleshooting Recipient Rate Limit (RRL)
